title: Reversible Bulk Photorelease of Strontium Ion from a Crown Ether-Linked Merocyanine
abstract:
668
¹ 2002 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1439-4235/02/03/08 $ 20.00+.50/0
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668 ±674
Reversible Bulk Photorelease of Strontium Ion
from a Crown Ether-Linked Merocyanine
Pascal Plaza,*[a] Isabelle Leray,[b] Pascale Changenet-Barret,[a]
Monique M. Martin,[a] and Bernard Valeur*[b, c]
The use of a crown-ether-linked merocyanine, DCM-crown, for
producing photoinduced, long-lived and reversible concentration
jumps of metal cations is reexamined. In this new investigation, the
excited-state behavior of DCM-crown complexed with a strontium
ion in acetonitrile is found to exhibit the same trends as those
previously reported with calcium and lithium ions. However, some
new features provide evidence for cation photorelease to the bulk.
Subpicosecond transient absorption experiments confirm the initial
fast photodisruption of the interaction between the ion and the
crown, and the formation of a loose complex after intramolecular
charge transfer within the chromophore. Two additional steps are
observed. Firstly, a continuous red shift of the gain spectrum is seen
on the subnanosecond scale. It is assigned to the movement of the
Sr2 cation away from the chromophore, partly to the bulk of the
solvent and partly towards the formation of an ultraloose complex
with oxygen atoms of the crown. Secondly, a free-ligand-like
absorption remains after the complete decay of the excited state.
This band, which signals a total photorelease of the Sr2 ion,
disappears with a characteristic time of about 110 ns, attributed to
the recomplexation of the crown in the ground state of the dye.
KEYWORDS:
cation photorelease ¥ DCM-crown ¥ picosecond dynamics ¥
strontium ¥ time-resolved spectroscopy
Fast and spatially controllable release of cations from a ™cage∫
upon irradiation by a light pulse is of great interest for the time-
resolved study of intracellular biological processes triggered by a
concentration jump of, for example, Ca2 or K. Several methods
have been proposed for photorelease, either irreversible or
reversible.
Photorelease of cations can be achieved by a change in the
chemical structure of chelators,[1] or photocleavage of chela-
tors[2±4] or cryptands.[5, 6] The major drawback of most of those
structures is that the cation photorelease is irreversible and does
not allow the repetition of the experiment over a large number
of triggering shots, for improving the signal-to-noise ratio by
accumulation.
In contrast, reversibility is expected from complexing mole-
cules possessing an azacrown, in which the nitrogen atom is
conjugated with an electron-withdrawing group, so that photo-
induced intramolecular charge transfer reduces the electron
density on the crown nitrogen atom. This atom thus becomes a
noncoordinating atom, positively polarised, which repulses the
cation. DCM-crown was the first promising example.[7] DCM-
crown consists of the well-known laser dye DCM (4-dicyano-
methylene-2-methyl-6-p-diethylaminostyryl-4H-pyran), in which
the dimethylamino group has been replaced by a macrocycle
(monoaza-[15]crown-5) that can bind metal ions (Scheme 1). The
nitrogen atom of the azacrown acts not only as an electron
donor atom in the complex but also as a donor involved in the
photoinduced charge transfer to the dicyanomethylene group.
Previous steady-state and time-resolved experiments[7±9] provid-
ed evidence for photoinduced disruption of the interaction
between the nitrogen atom of the crown and a bound cation in
DCM-crown complexes with Ca2 and Li. Other examples of
Scheme 1. Molecular structure of a DCM-crown complex with a Mn cation.
crown compounds have been reported for which the authors
also drew conclusions in terms of photodisruption resulting from
photoinduced charge transfer.[10±14] But in all these investigations
no evidence for true photoejection of the cation to the bulk was
provided. This question deserves further attention and is the
object of the present paper.
[a] Dr. P. Plaza, Dr. P. Changenet-Barret, Dr. M. M. Martin
Laboratoire P.A.S.T.E.U.R. (CNRS UMR 8640)
De¬partement de Chimie
Ecole Normale Supe¬rieure
24 rue Lhomond, 75231 Paris Cedex 05 (France)
Fax: (33)1443-23325
E-mail: Pascal.Plaza@ens.fr
[b] Prof. B. Valeur, Dr. I. Leray
Laboratoire P.P.S.M. (CNRS UMR 8531)
ENS-Cachan, 61 avenue du Pre¬sident Wilson
94235 Cachan Cedex (France)
[c] Prof. B. Valeur
Laboratoire de Chimie Ge¬ne¬rale
CNAM, 292 rue Saint Martin
75141 Paris Cedex 03 (France)
Fax: (33)1402-72362
E-mail: valeur@cnam.fr
 Reversible Bulk Photorelease from a Crown Ether
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668± 674
669
We face the question of whether the photodisruption of the
interaction between the bound cation and the crown nitrogen
atom leads to a true photoejection, that is, a long-lived cation
diffusion into the bulk. As far as transient absorption experi-
ments on Ca2 and Li complexes revealed,[9] no such photo-
ejection could be demonstrated. The present work is a new
investigation of the complex with the heavier and larger
strontium ion, in acetonitrile. The choice of this ion arose from
a joint project with the European Facility for Synchrotron
Radiation (ESRF) in Grenoble (France), which attempted to
directly observe the photoejection by time-resolved X-ray
scattering in solution.[15] For this purpose, an electron-rich cation
like strontium is preferable. We report here on the spectroscopic
time-resolved observation of a complete photocycle of DCM-
crown complexed with strontium, including ultrafast photo-
induced complex disruption, cation ejection to the bulk and, on
a much longer time scale, ground-state recomplexation.
Results and Discussion
Effects of Strontium Binding on DCM-crown Spectra and
Fluorescence Decay
As was previously published for DCM-crown complexation with
Li or Ca2, complexation of Sr2 significantly alters the steady-
state photophysical properties of the molecule. In the first place,
strontium binding induces a blue shift of the absorption
spectrum (Table 1) together with a decrease of the molar
absorption coefficient. Figure 1 illustrates the spectral changes,
along with the similar effects of Li and Ca2 complexation. This
Figure 1. Absorption () and corrected fluorescence (IFl) spectra of DCM-crown
and its complexes with Li (0.1 M), Ca2 (0.1 M) and Sr2 (0.1 M) in acetonitrile.
effect is explained by the attraction of the lone pair of the crown
nitrogen atom by the cation, which reduces its participation in
the delocalized  system of the chromophore. On the other
hand, fluorescence spectra do not show such drastic spectral
changes upon cation binding. The emission shape remains
mostly identical, but shows a slight blue shift of the maximum
(Figure 1). The fluorescence decay measured upon observation
beyond 630 nm is only slightly affected but the fluorescence
quantum yield is seen to be noticeably reduced by complexation
(Table 1). As was discussed in previous studies,[7±9] these
observations can be rationalised in terms of a photoinduced
decomplexation which produces, with a nonunity yield, a
fluorescent state essentially identical to the emitting state of
the free ligand. It can be noted here that these properties make
DCM-crown an effective fluorescent molecular sensor for cation
recognition.
The stability constant of the complex was determined by
monitoring the changes in absorbance or fluorescence intensity
on gradual addition of strontium salt. Analysis of the titration
curve confirmed the existence of a 1:1 complex whose stability
constant was found to be 437, that is logKs 2.64. This value is
slightly higher than with Li but one order of magnitude lower
than with Ca2. Such a difference is easily explained by both the
charge and the ionic diameter of the cation. The size of Li is
smaller than the crown cavity (diameter 1.7 ± 2.2 ä)[18] and
cannot interact efficiently with all the oxygen atoms. This leads
to a looser complex than with Ca2 which better fits the cavity
and, additionally, has a higher binding charge density. As for Sr2,
its diameter is larger than that of the cavity and forces it to sit
above the crown, reducing the stability of the complex.
As in the previous investigation of lithium and calcium
complexes, we examined the fluorescence decay at different
wavelengths using interferential Balzers filters (10 nm bandpass)
at 544 and 581 nm, and a Schott cut-off filter RG630 ( 
630 nm). The measurements were done in the presence of an
excess of Sr2 (0.1 M) so that the amount of free ligand was
negligible. Global analysis of the data showed a double
exponential decay at 544 and 581 nm: the long decay time is
2.03 0.03 ns, a value very close to those measured at  
630 nm (2.09 ns) and in the absence of cation (2.2 ns); the
second decay time is 0.48 0.06 ns with a preexponential factor
equal to 0.48  0.07 at 544 nm and 0.12 0.08 at 581 nm. The
relevant fractional intensities are 0.18 and 0.03, respectively.
These results are consistent with the previously measured short
decay times of 0.45 and 0.40 ns with Li and Ca2, respectively.
The species with a lifetime of 2 ns is assigned to the ligand
(either the free ligand or a complex in which the cation is no
longer in interaction with the nitrogen of the crown).
Initial Photodisruption of the Complex
Figure 2 shows the early times (2.5 to 20 ps) of the time-resolved
differential absorbance spectra of the DCM-crown: Sr2 complex
in acetonitrile. One of the main features of these spectra is a
large and broad negative band extending from 550 to 800 nm,
lying in the spectral domain of the steady-state fluorescence and
thus arising mainly from stimulated emission. This band under-

complexes in acetonitrile.

crown complexed with strontium, including ultrafast photo-
induced complex disruption, cation ejection to the bulk and, on
a much longer time scale, ground-state recomplexation.
Effects of Strontium Binding on DCM-crown Spectra and
As was previously published for DCM-crown complexation with

state photophysical properties of the molecule. In the first place,
strontium binding induces a blue shift of the absorption
spectrum (Table 1) together with a decrease of the molar
absorption coefficient. Figure 1 illustrates the spectral changes,
along with the similar effects of Li and Ca2 complexation. This

and its complexes with Li (0.1 M), Ca2 (0.1 M) and Sr2 (0.1 M) in acetonitrile.
effect is explained by the attraction of the lone pair of the crown
nitrogen atom by the cation, which reduces its participation in
the delocalized  system of the chromophore. On the other
hand, fluorescence spectra do not show such drastic spectral
changes upon cation binding. The emission shape remains
mostly identical, but shows a slight blue shift of the maximum
(Figure 1). The fluorescence decay measured upon observation
beyond 630 nm is only slightly affected but the fluorescence

(Table 1). As was discussed in previous studies,[7±9] these
observations can be rationalised in terms of a photoinduced
decomplexation which produces, with a nonunity yield, a
fluorescent state essentially identical to the emitting state of
the free ligand. It can be noted here that these properties make

monitoring the changes in absorbance or fluorescence intensity
on gradual addition of strontium salt. Analysis of the titration
curve confirmed the existence of a 1:1 complex whose stability
constant was found to be 437, that is logKs 2.64. This value is
slightly higher than with Li but one order of magnitude lower

As in the previous investigation of lithium and calcium
complexes, we examined the fluorescence decay at different
wavelengths using interferential Balzers filters (10 nm bandpass)
at 544 and 581 nm, and a Schott cut-off filter RG630 ( 
630 nm). The measurements were done in the presence of an
excess of Sr2 (0.1 M) so that the amount of free ligand was
negligible. Global analysis of the data showed a double
exponential decay at 544 and 581 nm: the long decay time is
2.03 0.03 ns, a value very close to those measured at  
630 nm (2.09 ns) and in the absence of cation (2.2 ns); the
second decay time is 0.48 0.06 ns with a preexponential factor
equal to 0.48  0.07 at 544 nm and 0.12 0.08 at 581 nm. The

(either the free ligand or a complex in which the cation is no
longer in interaction with the nitrogen of the crown).
Initial Photodisruption of the Complex
Figure 2 shows the early times (2.5 to 20 ps) of the time-resolved
differential absorbance spectra of the DCM-crown: Sr2 complex
in acetonitrile. One of the main features of these spectra is a
large and broad negative band extending from 550 to 800 nm,
lying in the spectral domain of the steady-state fluorescence and
thus arising mainly from stimulated emission. This band under-
Table 1. Photophysical properties of DCM-crown and its Li, Ca2 and Sr2
absorption emission Fluorescence
Quantum Yield [ns] (  630 nm)
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668 ±674
Figure 2. Early differential absorbance spectra (A) of DCM-crown (2.7 104 M )
in acetonitrile in the presence of 0.1 M Sr2. Delay times span from 2.5 ps (after the
signal rise due to pump ± probe overlap) to 20 ps.
goes a very fast modification on both sides of a temporary
isosbestic point (587 nm). The signal is seen to rise on the blue
side of the isosbestic point, while it becomes more negative on
the red side. A slight red shift of the band maximum (from 613 to
615 nm) is also observed. This behaviour clearly suggests an
excited-state reaction involving two emissive states in a
precursor ± successor relationship. This reaction is equally ob-
served in the transient absorption domain (positive band
between 420 and 550 nm) where a second temporary isosbestic
point (509 nm) is seen during the same period of time.
Those general trends have also been reported for DCM-crown
complexes with Li and Ca2[9] and are actually a spectrodynamic
signature of the free ligand. Indeed, it has been shown that both
DCM-crown and its well-studied parent molecule DCM exhibit a
similar type of excited-state behaviour, which was interpreted as
a charge transfer reaction[9, 19±21] leading to a very polar
(26 D)[22, 23] emissive excited state. The excited complex spectrally
behaves very much like the free ligand, meaning that a
disruption of the bond linking the cation to the nitrogen atom
shared by the crown and the chromophore takes place either
during the optical transition or in a time shorter than our
experimental resolution (1.5 ps). A further element in favour of
the photodisruption hypothesis is given by the close identity of
the bleaching-compensated (pure excited-state absorption co-
efficients) spectra we extracted from our data at delay times
where only the first reacting state is present. Figure 3 displays
those pure excited-state spectra for DCM-crown:Sr2 and DCM-
crown:Ca2 compared to that of DCM in tetrahydrofuran. A good
agreement is reached in the absorption region and to a lesser
extent in the stimulated emission domain, possibly due to the
large error of the extraction method.[9, 17]
One point of special importance is that the early dynamics of
the DCM-crown complexes with Sr2, as well as with Li or Ca2,
are much slower than what is observed for the free ligand or
DCM in acetonitrile. Figure 4 shows the decay or rise kinetic
traces taken at selected wavelengths (468, 538 and 618 nm),
which in the case of Sr2 could be well fitted with a 9 ps
exponential. This time has to be compared with 2 ps for Li,
Figure 3. Excited-state absorption coefficients (trans.) of DCM-crown complexes
in acetonitrile calculated[9, 17] from the differential absorbance spectra at 1 ps for
Sr2 and 2 ps for Ca2. The spectra are compared to that of DCM in THF extracted
from differential absorbance data at 0.5 ps.[9]
Figure 4. Kinetic traces of the differential absorbance (A) data of DCM-crown:
Sr2 at selected wavelengths in the stimulated emission and the transient
absorption domains: 468, 538 and 618 nm. The data are fitted by a 9 ps
exponential convoluted by a 1.5 ps (FWHM) Gaussian representing the exper-
imental response function. The time shift in the initial rise is due to the chirp of the
30 ps for Ca2 and especially 1 ± 2 ps for the free ligand.[9] As
previously reported, we interpret this slowing of the kinetics
using the above-mentioned charge transfer hypothesis. After the
initial photodisruption of the cation ± nitrogen bond, the Sr2 ion
still remains very close to the amino end of the chromophore,
electrostatically interacting with it and hampering the charge
transfer process that has to bring a significant positive charge to
the nitrogen of the crown. For this to happen, a movement of
the Sr2 ion away from the nitrogen atom must occur, and we
believe this is described by the reaction time we measured.
Consistently, one can note that those times scale well with the
ground-state association constants of the complexes (Table 2),
supporting the idea that the initial photodisplacement of the
cation strongly depends on the interaction strength in the
ground state, and thus on the charge and size of the cation. This
step of the photoinduced reaction might be related to the report
by Lapouyade and co-workers of a solvent-separated cation ±
Reversible Bulk Photorelease from a Crown Ether
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668± 674
probe pair intermediate in the excited-state pathway of a crown-
phenylstilbene derivative with Ca2.[10±12] It should be stressed
that this cation displacement is not thought of as a genuine
photoejection but rather the formation of an excited-state loose
complex that further evolves, as we will see in the next Section.
Bulk Photoejection in the Excited State
Figure 5 shows the time-resolved differential absorbance spectra
of DCM-crown: Sr2 in acetonitrile for pump ± probe delays from
20 ps to 1.5 ns. For this time range, one would expect to observe
the same type of decay as the free DCM-crown or the complexes
with Li or Ca2, that is, mainly a standard radiative relaxation of
Figure 5. Differential absorbance (A) spectra of DCM-crown (2.7 104 M) in
acetonitrile in the presence of 0.1 M Sr2 for delay times spanning from 20 ps to
the free ligand fluorescent charge transfer state. This is in fact
what is basically seen here, with all bands decaying to zero with
a 1.5 ± 2 ns decay time. Nevertheless, a completely new feature is
superimposed on this simple picture. One can clearly identify a
continuous red shift of the stimulated emission band as it
declines, its peak starting at 615 nm and ending at 628 nm
(Figure 5). We plot in Figure 6 the time dependence of this shift
in an energy scale, satisfactorily fitted by a 400 ps exponential
This kind of shift cannot be explained by a specific DCM or
DCM-crown intrinsic behaviour nor by an experimental temper-
ature or pressure jump artifact, since the effect is only observed
in the case of the strontium complex. A solvation mechanism
Figure 6. Red-shift kinetics of the gain (stimulated emission) band peak ( gainpeak)
of DCM-crown:Sr2. The 50 cm1 error bars reflect the precision of the band
peak determination. The decay is fitted by a single exponential function with a
characteristic time of 400 ps.
could be a better explanation, for it is known to induce such
dynamical Stokes shifts.[24] Pure acetonitrile relaxation cannot be
invoked since its average solvation time is extremely short (260
fs).[24] In electrolyte solutions, much longer relaxation times have
been reported, in relation to the exchange of a solvent molecule
of the first solvation shell by a cation from the bulk.[25, 26] After
carefully scaling the results of Chapman et al.[25] to a 0.1 M
strontium concentration, it nevertheless appears that the short-
est lived of their components is still three times longer than our
finding, which rules the hypothesis out.
We must then consider a different explanation that can be
found in the nearby presence of the initially bound cation and its
electrostatic interaction with the polar excited state of the
chromophore. We propose here that the strontium cation–
whose bonding to its nitrogen anchor was disrupted upon
excitation and it was then pushed slightly aside in a loose
complex configuration (by the intramolecular charge transfer
within the chromophore)–moves further away from the nitro-
gen atom of the crown during this 400 ps process. One can think
of it as a fading of the repulsive electrostatic interaction between
the cation and this nitrogen atom by both a distance effect and a
screening by intervening solvent molecules.
Following this hypothesis, the question arises: Where does the
cation go? One can imagine it could stay around the crown,
possibly bound to it in a kind of looser complex with oxygen
atoms that we will name ∫ultraloose complex∫. Alternatively, it
may completely detach from the crown and drift into the bulk,
being independently solvated by acetonitrile molecules. Such a
complete photoejection is in fact to be considered with special
attention, because the stability constant of the complex in the
excited state is smaller than in the ground state, since the
nitrogen atom of the crown is no longer a coordinating atom. A
fraction of the bound cations of the excited complexes is thus
expected to be photoejected to the bulk. Both processes would
not be discernible by the results we discussed up to this point
and could even coexist. Further experiments will now be
described to clarify this point.
Table 2. Sizes of several cations and association constants with DCM-crown
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668 ±674
The bulk photoejection alternative proposed in the preceding
Section has an important corollary. If this mechanism were true,
one would expect that, after a time longer than the fluorescence
lifetime (about 2 ns), part of the initially excited loose complexes
would end up as free ligands in the ground state. Spectroscopi-
cally, this situation would show up as a differential absorbance
spectrum having a positive contribution from the free ligand
absorption and a negative contribution from the complex. From
Figure 1, one can readily calculate the positive band maximum at
474 nm, the negative band around 400 nm and a zero value, the
isosbestic point between the two species, at 427 nm.
We proceeded to check this consequence of our hypothesis
using two electronically synchronised lasers for pumping and
probing the sample with very long delay times. Figure 7 shows
that after the 2 ns decay of the excited-state signal a remaining
spectrum appears (displayed here at 10 ns). Figure 8 gives a
Figure 7. Differential absorbance (A) spectra of DCM-crown:Sr2 obtained with
two synchronised (2 ns) subpicosecond lasers. The chosen delay times are: 5,
0 (pump and probe overlap), 3 and 10 ns.
Figure 8. Nanosecond differential absorbance (A) spectra of DCM-crown:Sr2.
Delay times span from 10 to 1000 ns. Inset: Kinetics at 480 nm fitted to a 110 
26 ns exponential function (plus a constant).
closer view of this spectrum (accumulated over 1000 pump shots
to increase sensitivity) and shows its decay on a submicrosecond
timescale. One can recognise an absorption band around
(474 nm). The inset in Figure 8 shows a kinetic trace recorded
at 480 nm. It can be fitted by a 110 26 ns exponential function.
An alternative interpretation of this spectrum and kinetics
would be that the free-ligand-like excited state relaxes partly to
its triplet state that decays by oxygen quenching with the 110 ns
time constant. Although the very low triplet yield reported for
DCM (in methanol: 3  103)[23] could be enhanced by a heavy
atom effect, the T1 Tn absorption spectrum reported by Meyer
et al.[23] is mostly flat in the visible region with a shallow
maximum at 530 nm; the absorbance is seen to rise again above
700 nm. This description does not fit our observation.
Another possibility could be that the nanosecond signal
comes from an isomerized cis-species that can be formed in the
first picoseconds, in competition with the charge transfer
reaction. This mechanism was in fact invoked to explain the
variation of the nonradiative decay rate of the parent molecule
DCM as a function of the solvent polarity:[27] When the solvent is
polar, the charge transfer reaction is fast and does not allow
much isomerisation but, when the polarity is reduced, the
charge transfer reaction slows down and some isomerisation can
take place in the locally excited state. In the case of DCM-crown
complexes, photolysis experiments (not shown here) under a
continuous UV lamp evidenced a stable photoproduct assigned
to the cis-isomer. This species absorbs on the blue side of the
trans-band with a maximum around 343 nm for the strontium
complex. By no means can this band be compared to the
metastable 480 nm species reported here. Ruling those two
interpretations out, we confirm our proposal that the photo-
ejection of strontium from the crown leads to a nanosecond-
lifetime free ligand in the ground state.
The yield of the complete photoejection process (cation in the
bulk and ligand in the ground state) can be very roughly
estimated to 5 ± 10%, taking 4.3 104 M1cm1 (value obtained
from Figure 1 data) as the differential absorption coefficient of
the 480 nm band and assuming a 10% pumping rate (value
obtained from the bleaching compensation technique referred
to in the Section ™Initial Photodisruption...∫). This small value
implies that most of the excited-state population (loose complex
in the charge transfer state) takes a different pathway and we
suppose it stays in those ultraloose complexes we alluded to in
the preceding Section, in which the strontium ion has stayed
close to the crown without actually being released to the bulk.
Those ultraloose complexes would thus undergo a geminate
recombination in the ground state on a shorter timescale than
the excited-state lifetime. Geminate recombination is likely to be
a more efficient deactivation channel of the Ca2 complex we
studied previously, and for which experimental evidence for
photoejection has not yet been obtained.[9] The reason might
again be that Ca2 fits tightly the crown cavity and is less easily
released in the excited state. Sr2, which is forced to sit on top of
the crown, has more facility to leave the ligand surroundings
before the chromophore returns to the ground state and
recovers its full binding capability.
Reversible Bulk Photorelease from a Crown Ether
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 668± 674
The last aspect of our observation is the 110 ns lifetime of the
free ligand in the ground state. Within the framework of our
interpretation, this is clearly due to a recomplexation mechanism
restoring the initial equilibrium of the solution. It is interesting to
note that, with a strontium concentration of 0.1 M and taking a
diffusion-limited bimolecular rate constant of 2 1010 M1s1
(from the approximate formula 8RT/3[28]), the fastest complex-
ation time possible should be of the order of 0.5 ns. Since the
kinetics we observed are two orders of magnitude slower, we
think that the recomplexation process is not diffusion limited
and that we measured an intrinsic rate constant of complex-
ation, namely 9 107 M1s1. This rate compares very well with
the reported value for [15]crown-5 association with Sr2 in water,
6.5 107.[29] Similarly, Na and K association rate constants (with
[15]crown-5 in water) are 2.6 108 and 4.3 108 M1s1,[30]
respectively. A last element of comparison can be provided by
the rate constant of 7  107 M1s1 measured by Lednev et al. for
the association of Ba2 with a benzothiaziolium styryl azacrown
This work provides new developments to former studies of
metal cation photorelease by DCM-crown. As in the case of Li
and Ca2 complexes, a disruption of the cation binding followed
by a first movement of the cation out of the crown, accompany-
ing the intramolecular charge transfer within the chromophore,
is observed for the DCM-crown:Sr2 complex. These steps lead to
an excited-state loose complex which continues to evolve. A
large movement of the Sr2 cation away from the nitrogen atom
of the crown is seen in the subnanosecond regime while the
chromophore is still in the charge transfer excited state. A small
part of this movement is thought to go all the way to the bulk of
the solvent while a more important fraction of it leads to a kind of
ultraloose complex with oxygen atoms that, eventually, under-
goes a fast geminate recombination in the ground state. On a
longer time scale, the small ejected part of the excited population
ends up as a free ligand in the ground state which, ultimately,
reassociates with bulk Sr2 ion on a submicrosecond timescale.
This photoinduced cascade of reactions is particularly inter-
esting for the design of ligands able to bind and reversibly
photorelease biologically relevant metal cations. To our knowl-
edge, no such complete photocycle, including a long-lived
nonequilibrium decomplexed state, was previously demonstrat-
ed. A further check of our proposal has been planned at the ESRF
in Grenoble, using time-resolved X-ray scattering in solution to
directly monitor the movements of the atoms. The experiments
DCM-crown was synthesised by as previously reported.[7] A 2.7
104 M solution in acetonitrile (Sigma Aldrich spectrometric grade)
was used for all experiments. About 98% complexation of Sr2 ions
was obtained by dissolving 0.1 M of strontium perchlorate 99.9%
Absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary 5E (Varian) spectropho-
tometer. Corrected emission spectra were obtained on an spectro-
fluorometer (SPEX 1681 Fluorolog). The fluorescence decay times
were measured on a multifrequency phase-modulation fluorometer
(SPEX Fluorolog 3). The laser dye DCM was used as a reference
Transient absorption and gain experiments were performed by the
pump± probe with continuum technique using a subpicosecond
laser as the source. The laser is a non-mode-locked homemade dye
laser described elsewhere.[16] It is driven by a single seeded 10 Hz
Q-switched Nd:YAG laser and delivers intense 500 fs pulses at
610 nm. This beam is used to produce a continuum of white light in a
water cell which, in turn, is filtered and amplified in two separate,
synchronous, amplification chains. Two subpicosecond wavelengths
were then simultaneously generated, at 570 nm with Rhodamine 6G
as the gain medium and at 428 nm with Stilbene3.
A detailed description of the main features of our pump± probe
setup has already been published.[17] Pumping was done with the
428 nm beam while the 570 nm beam generated a continuum that
was used as the probe. For less than two nanosecond pump± probe
delays, the probe beam was retarded with respect to the pump
beam in an optical delay line driven by a stepper motor. For longer
delays, two separate subpicosecond lasers were used and electroni-
cally synchronised (jitter 2 ns). One delivered 610 nm pulses for
generating the continuum probe while the other delivered 428 nm
pulses for pumping. The probe beam was split into a sample and a
reference channel to compensate for energy and spectrum fluctua-
tions. Pump and probe beams crossed on the sample, held in a 1 mm
cuvette, at an angle of about 10 and were set at the magic
polarisation angle. The sample solution (50 mL) was recirculated at
room temperature, to avoid significant photolysis during the time of
the experiment; it was not deaerated. The transmitted beams were
collected with 600 m optical fibers and sent to a spectrograph
(Jobin-Yvon 270M; grating: 100 groovesmm1, entrance slit: 64 m).
Spectra were recorded on a 1024 128 pixel CCD (Princeton
Instruments) and accumulated over 500 pump shots.
Time-resolved differential absorbance spectra (A; absorbance of
the pumped sample minus steady-state absorbance) were calculated
and corrected from the group velocity dispersion in the probe beam,
which was measured in a separate experiment by two-photon
absorption in pure 1-chloronaphthalene. The experimental error on
the differential absorbance measurements was estimated to be 
5 103 OD. The time resolution of the experiments was given a
posteriori by fitting the data at selected wavelengths with expo-
nentials convoluted by a Gaussian representing the experimental
response function. The FWHM of this Gaussian was found to be 1.5 
